Title: Glycogen Metabolic Genes Are Involved in Trehalose-6-Phosphate Synthase-Mediated Regulation of Pathogenicity by the Rice Blast Fungus Magnaporthe oryzae

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Trehalose-6-phosphate synthase complex component; glucose-6-phosphate and NADPH-responsive switch protein
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Storage carbohydrate and regulator-associated metabolite linked to fungal virulence
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in wild type mycelium; reduced in Δagl1, Δgph1, and Δagl1Δgph1
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in wild type mycelium; reduced in glycogen-catabolism mutants
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression is strongly reduced when AGL1 and/or GPH1 are deleted, linking glycogen breakdown to the Tps1-mediated virulence switch
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: qRT-PCR expression analysis; genetic interaction inferred from NMR3 deletion rescue
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Vegetative growth in complete medium; interpretation extended to infection-related development
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TPS1 transcript abundance decreases about 5-fold in glycogen catabolic mutants. 2. Reduced TPS1 expression correlates with reduced trehalose and reduced pathogenicity. 3. The data support TPS1 as the mechanistic bridge between glycogen metabolism and virulence regulation.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: TPS1 is central mechanistically but not directly manipulated in this paper; included as a core common gene because the study explicitly measures its expression and builds the causal model around it.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate antifungal target because the paper supports its central role in virulence-associated metabolic regulation.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Storage carbohydrate linked to fungal virulence-associated metabolism
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in wild type mycelium; reduced in Δagl1, Δgph1, and especially Δagl1Δgph1
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in wild type mycelium; reduced in glycogen-catabolism mutants
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression is markedly reduced in glycogen catabolic mutants, accompanying lower trehalose levels
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TPS3 expression declines about 6-fold in Δagl1, 9-fold in Δgph1, and 50-fold in Δagl1Δgph1. 2. Reduced TPS3 expression accompanies lower trehalose accumulation in glycogen-metabolic mutants. 3. TPS3 supports the conclusion that glycogen degradation influences the trehalose biosynthetic machinery.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Measured as a mechanistic readout rather than directly mutated in this study.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential supportive engineering target in metabolic studies, though direct virulence-function testing was not performed here.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Glycogen degradation linked to trehalose metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Alpha-amylase superfamily protein with conserved α/β barrel domain motifs
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes α-1,6 branch linkages in glycogen to release glucose during glycogen debranching
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Downstream glycogen catabolic step in cytosolic mobilization
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool and supports regulation of trehalose pathway genes
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key glycogen-mobilizing enzyme for pathogenicity-associated glycogen degradation; rate-limiting not formally established
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Storage carbohydrate and part of the Tps1-associated virulence regulatory system
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Detected in conidia, germ tubes, appressoria, and invasive hyphae
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected in conidia, germ tubes, appressoria, and invasive hyphae
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for efficient glycogen mobilization and full pathogenicity; deletion reduces trehalose levels and decreases TPS1/TPS3 expression
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Targeted gene deletion; enzyme activity assay; GFP localization; glycogen staining; glycogen quantification; pathogenicity assay; qRT-PCR; complementation
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Appressorium development on hydrophobic surface under nutrient-free conditions; plant infection in rice and barley
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AGL1 encodes a cytoplasmic amyloglucosidase required for efficient glycogen mobilization during appressorium development. 2. Δagl1 mutants retain glycogen longer, show reduced trehalose accumulation, and exhibit ~5-fold reduced TPS1 and ~6-fold reduced TPS3 expression. 3. Loss of AGL1 reduces lesion formation and impairs invasive hyphal growth in host tissue.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Δagl1 mutants showed normal conidial germination, appressorium formation, appressorium turgor, and penetration peg formation, indicating the main virulence defect is during invasive growth rather than penetration. Native-gene complementation restored growth on starch.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential antifungal target because disrupting glycogen breakdown diminishes virulence without blocking appressorium formation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Deletion impairs utilization of glycogen and starch as sole carbon sources and reduces invasive growth.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Glycogen degradation linked to trehalose metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Glycogen phosphorylase family; conserved pyridoxal-5'-phosphate binding site
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Phosphorolytically cleaves α-1,4 glucosidic bonds from non-reducing ends of glycogen to release glucose-1-phosphate
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Core glycogen catabolic step in cytosolic mobilization
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool and supports regulation of trehalose pathway genes
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key glycogen-mobilizing enzyme for pathogenicity-associated glycogen degradation; rate-limiting not formally established
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Storage carbohydrate and part of the Tps1-associated virulence regulatory system
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Detected in conidia, germ tubes, appressoria, and invasive hyphae
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected in conidia, germ tubes, appressoria, and invasive hyphae
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for full pathogenicity and normal glycogen utilization; deletion reduces trehalose levels and decreases TPS1/TPS3 expression
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Targeted gene deletion; enzyme activity assay; GFP localization; glycogen staining; glycogen quantification; pathogenicity assay; qRT-PCR; complementation
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Appressorium development on hydrophobic surface under nutrient-free conditions; plant infection in rice and barley
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GPH1 encodes a cytoplasmic glycogen phosphorylase needed for glycogen catabolism and normal growth on glycogen/starch. 2. Δgph1 mutants accumulate glycogen in mycelia on minimal medium, show reduced trehalose accumulation, and exhibit ~5-fold reduced TPS1 and ~9-fold reduced TPS3 expression. 3. Loss of GPH1 reduces lesion formation and impairs invasive hyphal proliferation in host cells.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Glycogen mobilization defect in Δgph1 appressoria was milder than in Δagl1, possibly due to compensation by glucoamylase activity. Mutants retained normal germination, appressorium formation, turgor, and penetration.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential antifungal target for reducing blast virulence by disrupting glycogen catabolism and associated virulence regulation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Deletion impairs utilization of glycogen and starch as sole carbon sources and reduces invasive growth.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Glycogen synthase family with conserved C-terminal regulatory phosphorylation sites
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose from activated sugar donor to elongate α-1,4 glucan chains during glycogen synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Core anabolic step in glycogen storage synthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Affects storage pool but not required for pathogenicity-associated terminal outcome in this study
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key glycogen synthesis enzyme; not a pathogenicity control step in this study
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Intracellular carbon and energy storage reserve
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for normal intracellular glycogen synthesis but dispensable for pathogenicity
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Targeted gene deletion; glycogen staining; glycogen quantification; pathogenicity assay
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Appressorium development and infection on rice and barley
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GSN1 encodes glycogen synthase and its deletion severely reduces glycogen accumulation in conidia and appressoria. 2. Despite strong glycogen depletion, Δgsn1 mutants retain normal pathogenicity on rice and barley. 3. Glycogen storage itself is therefore dispensable for blast infection under the tested conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The text names MGG_07289.6, whereas a supporting figure caption lists MGG_07829.6; accession inconsistency noted from the paper. This gene was included because it is a primary experimental subject in Results.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Low value as a standalone antifungal target for disease control because reducing glycogen synthesis did not reduce virulence.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Deletion strongly lowers glycogen storage but no pathogenicity penalty was observed.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Tps1-associated virulence gene regulation linked to carbon/nitrogen metabolism and trehalose-associated signaling
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Negative regulation of virulence-associated gene expression downstream of the Tps1 metabolic switch
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Acts as inhibitor in the Tps1-dependent regulatory network; deletion relieves repression
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Deletion partially restores virulence to the Δagl1Δgph1 mutant, showing NMR3 is a decisive negative component of the glycogen/Tps1 regulatory axis
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Part of an NADPH-dependent Tps1 regulatory switch; specific feedback not directly proven for NMR3 alone
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Tps1-mediated sensing of glucose-6-phosphate and NADPH/NADP balance
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Trehalose pathway components are linked to pathogenicity regulation in the fungus
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Deletion of NMR3 partially restores pathogenicity to the Δagl1Δgph1 mutant
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Targeted gene deletion in double-mutant background; pathogenicity assay on rice and barley
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Plant infection assays on rice cultivar CO-39 and barley cultivar Golden Promise
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NMR3 functions as a negative regulator in the Tps1-associated metabolic gene switch. 2. Removing NMR3 in the Δagl1Δgph1 background restores lesion formation close to wild type. 3. This supports a model in which glycogen catabolism promotes virulence through the NADPH-dependent Tps1/Nmr regulatory system.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Associated with the Tps1-regulated Nmr inhibitory system
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The study directly tests NMR3 because prior work showed deleting NMR genes restores pathogenicity to Δtps1 mutants; here it validates linkage between glycogen metabolism and the Tps1 switch.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential regulatory target for modulating fungal virulence, though deletion increases pathogenicity in the defective mutant background and is therefore not a direct control target.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Deletion restores virulence in a glycogen-catabolism-defective background, indicating loss of NMR3 could enhance disease severity rather than suppress it.
